title: How to Use Intuition When Making Travel Decisions
meta_description: Trust your gut to guide your travels. This article shows how to use intuition for smarter trip decisions, not overthinking.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Travel Decisions
og_description: Trust your gut to guide your travels. This article shows how to use intuition for smarter trip decisions, not overthinking.
body:
You Already Know Where You're Supposed to Go

Here's the thing about travel decisions that nobody tells you: your gut already knows the answer before your brain starts making spreadsheets. You feel it in your chest when you look at a flight to somewhere you've never been. You get that weird electric tingle when a destination name keeps showing up in your feed. And you've probably ignored it a hundred times because it didn't make logical sense.

I'm Kalesh, and I've spent years working with people who feel everything too much. The kind of people who walk into a room and know something's off before anyone speaks. The kind who get homesick for places they've never visited. You're one of those people, aren't you? Right?! You know exactly what I'm talking about.

Here's what I want you to hear: that sensitivity isn't a glitch. It's your navigation system. And when it comes to travel decisions, your intuition is the most reliable guide you've got. Not because it's magical (though it can feel that way), but because it's processing information your conscious mind can't access. Does that land?

Let's talk about how to actually use that when you're deciding where to go, when to leave, and how to get there.

Why Your Brain Lying to You About Travel Is Normal

Your brain is wired for safety, not adventure. It wants you to stay where the food is predictable and the bed is familiar. So when you start thinking about a trip, your brain immediately throws up objections: "That's too expensive," "You don't speak the language," "What if you get sick," "What if you hate it."

Those aren't real reasons. Those are fear responses dressed up as logic. And here's the tricky part - they sound completely reasonable. Your brain is really good at making fear feel like wisdom.

But your intuition doesn't argue. It doesn't make lists. It just gives you a feeling. A knowing. A pull. And that pull is what you need to learn to trust, especially when your brain is screaming at you to stay home.

The Difference Between Fear and Intuition

This is where most people get stuck. How do you know if it's intuition or just anxiety? Here's the simple test:

  • Fear feels tight, constricted, and panicked. It's a loud voice that says "something bad will happen."
  • Intuition feels calm, clear, and certain. It's a quiet voice that says "this is right" even if you can't explain why.
  • Fear keeps you stuck. Intuition moves you forward, even if it's scary.
  • Fear is about control. Intuition is about trust.

When you're making travel decisions, pay attention to the quality of the feeling. If your stomach is in knots and you can't breathe, that's fear. If your chest feels warm and open, even while your brain is listing reasons not to go, that's intuition.

Your intuition doesn't need to explain itself. It just knows. And the more you trust it, the more it shows up for you.

How to Check In With Your Body Before Booking Anything

You can't use your intuition if you're not grounded in your body. Most people live in their heads - planning, worrying, analyzing. That's where fear lives. Intuition lives in your body. In your gut. In your chest. In that weird feeling at the back of your neck.

Before you book anything, take 60 seconds to do this:

Close your eyes. Put your hand on your belly. Take three slow breaths. Now imagine the destination you're considering. Don't think about it - feel it. What happens in your body? Does your belly relax or tighten? Does your chest feel open or closed? Do your shoulders drop or rise?

That's your answer. It's that simple. Your body will tell you yes or no before your brain finishes its first objection.

I had a student once who was trying to decide between two trips - a beach vacation in Mexico and a hiking trip in Scotland. Her brain had all these reasons for Mexico: cheaper flights, better weather, easier. But every time she pictured Scotland, her whole body relaxed. She almost cried. She went to Scotland, and it changed her life. Her body knew before she did.

You have that same knowing. You just have to stop overriding it.

When the Pull Feels Irrational, Trust It Even More

Here's where it gets interesting. Sometimes your intuition will pull you toward something that makes no sense. A random city you've never thought about. A trip that's inconveniently timed. A destination your friends think is weird.

That's when you really need to pay attention. Because when the pull feels irrational, it's usually your deeper self trying to get your attention.

I remember booking a flight to a small town in Portugal because I kept seeing the name everywhere - in books, on social media, in conversations with strangers. I had no reason to go there. I didn't know anyone. I couldn't even pronounce the name. But something in me said "go." So I did.

I ended up meeting someone who became a close friend. I found a cafe where I wrote half a book. I had experiences I still can't fully explain. None of it was planned. None of it made sense on paper. But it was exactly what I needed.

Your intuition works the same way. It doesn't show you the whole picture. It just shows you the next step. And sometimes that step looks ridiculous from the outside. Trust it anyway.

What to Do When Your Intuition Says No

Sometimes the pull is away, not toward. You get a bad feeling about a trip, and you can't explain why. Maybe it's a destination that should be amazing, but your body says no. Maybe it's a flight that feels wrong. Maybe it's a travel companion you're unsure about.

Listen to that no. It's just as important as the yes.

I had a student who was planning a trip to Thailand. Everything was booked. She was excited. But two weeks before leaving, she started feeling sick every time she thought about the flight. Not anxious - physically ill. She almost ignored it because she didn't want to lose the money. But she finally listened and canceled.

A week later, there was a political situation in the region she would have been in. She would have been stuck. Her body knew before the news did.

That's not to say every no is predicting disaster. Sometimes it's just not the right time. Sometimes it's not the right energy. Sometimes you're being redirected to something better. Trust the no as much as the yes.

Practical Ways to Let Intuition Guide Your Travel Decisions

Okay, so you're on board with the idea. But how do you actually do this in real life, when you're staring at flight prices and hotel reviews and wondering if you're making a mistake?

Here are some practical methods that work:

1. The Destination Test

Write down five destinations you're considering. Without thinking, rank them from 1 to 5 based on how they feel in your body. Not based on budget, time of year, or what's practical. Just pure feeling. The one that feels most alive in your body is your answer, even if it's the most inconvenient choice.

2. The Booking Check

Right before you hit "book" on anything - flight, hotel, tour - pause. Take a breath. Ask yourself: "Does this feel right?" If you get a clear yes in your body, book it. If you get a no, don't. If you get nothing, wait. The answer will come.

3. The Dream Clue

Pay attention to your dreams before and during travel planning. Your subconscious talks to you through dreams. If you dream about a specific place, that's a sign. If you dream about missing a flight or losing your luggage, that might be anxiety or a warning. Learn to tell the difference.

4. The Synchronicity Test

When you're considering a destination, start noticing signs. Do you keep hearing about it? Are people mentioning it? Do you see articles or posts about it? Synchronicities are your intuition speaking through the world around you.

One of the best books I've found for understanding how to work with these kinds of signs is The Celestine Prophecy by James Redfield. It's a story about following synchronicities and trusting the process. If you want to go deeper into how the universe talks to you through coincidences, See on Amazon (paid link).

What to Do When Your Intuition and Logic Conflict

This is the hard part. Sometimes your intuition says go, and your logic says stay. Sometimes your intuition says no, and your logic says you've already spent the money. What do you do?

First, don't ignore either one. Both are giving you information. Logic tells you about practical constraints. Intuition tells you about energetic alignment. You need both.

But here's the thing: practical constraints can usually be worked around. You can find cheaper flights. You can change dates. You can cancel and lose a deposit. Money can be replaced. Time can't.

Energy alignment is harder to fix. If you force yourself into a trip that doesn't feel right, you'll spend the whole time fighting yourself. You'll be tense. You'll miss the magic. You'll come back more tired than when you left.

So when they conflict, I always say: trust the intuition first, then figure out the practical stuff. You'd be surprised how often the logistics work out when you're aligned with what's right for you.

A Note on Travel Anxiety and Sensitive People

If you're a highly sensitive person, travel can be overwhelming. New places, new people, new sensory input - it's a lot. Your nervous system is already calibrated for a wider range than most people's. So travel hits you harder.

That doesn't mean you shouldn't travel. It means you need to travel differently. You need more downtime. You need to honor your limits. You need to listen when your body says "I need a rest day" even if you're in Paris and you feel like you should be seeing everything.

Your intuition will also guide you toward the right pace. Some trips are for adventure. Some are for restoration. Some are for both. Trust what you need in the moment, not what you think you should be doing.

For sensitive travelers, I recommend The Highly Sensitive Person by Elaine Aron. It's not a travel book, but it will help you understand your nervous system and how to care for it in new environments. See on Amazon (paid link).

How to Strengthen Your Travel Intuition Over Time

Intuition is like a muscle. The more you use it, the stronger it gets. Every time you trust a gut feeling and it works out, you build confidence. Every time you ignore it and regret it, you learn.

Here's how to practice:

  • Start small. Before you choose a restaurant or a walking route, check in with your body. Let intuition guide mundane decisions. This builds the habit.
  • Keep a travel journal. Write down the gut feelings you had and what happened. Over time, you'll see patterns. You'll learn the language of your own intuition.
  • Ask for guidance before you sleep. Say "show me where I need to go" and pay attention to dreams, morning feelings, and first thoughts.
  • Practice with photos. Look at pictures of places you've never been. Notice which ones make you feel something. That's your intuition responding to energy.

One book that changed how I think about this is The Alchemist by Paulo Coelho. It's a story about following your personal legend - the path your soul wants you to take. It's full of wisdom about trusting the journey even when you can't see the destination. See on Amazon (paid link).

The One Thing Nobody Tells You About Intuitive Travel

Here's the truth that most teachers won't say out loud: sometimes your intuition will lead you into discomfort. Not danger - discomfort. Growth is uncomfortable. Transformation is uncomfortable. And travel is one of the fastest ways to grow.

So when you trust your intuition and end up in a situation that's hard - a language barrier, a lost reservation, a difficult hike - that doesn't mean you made the wrong choice. It means you're in the middle of the process. The magic is on the other side of that discomfort.

Your intuition doesn't promise easy. It promises right. And right is almost always worth it.

I've had trips that were hard from start to finish. Missed connections, bad weather, illness. But every single one of them taught me something I needed to learn. Every single one rearranged something in me that needed rearranging. I wouldn't trade a single difficult trip for an easy one.

That's the paradox. The trips that feel wrong in the moment are often the ones that change you most. And your intuition knew that all along.

Final Thoughts Before You Book Your Next Trip

You already know where you're supposed to go. That quiet pull you keep feeling? That's not random. That's your soul telling you where the next lesson is, where the next gift is, where the next version of you is waiting.

Stop asking permission. Stop waiting for the perfect time. Stop letting fear make your decisions.

Book the trip that makes your chest tight with excitement. Say yes to the destination that keeps appearing in your peripheral vision. Trust the no when it comes, and trust the yes even harder.

Your intuition is never wrong. It's just waiting for you to listen.

If you want to go deeper into understanding your intuitive gifts and how they show up in your life, I've put together a short assessment that can help you identify your natural intuitive style. It takes about five minutes, and it might show you things about yourself you've never noticed.

Take the intuitive style assessment here and start trusting yourself more fully.
